text: Hi, my name is Dr. Robert Richard and what you're about to see is how we perform a robotic vertical sleeve gastrectomy. This video is relatively unedited and will give you a good idea of how we perform this operation. If you'd like more information, you can certainly contact us at our office or visit our website at thelongstreetclinic.com.
The da Vinci XI robotic system is docked, with two robotic arms positioned from the patient's right side. The liver is retracted, and the assistant operates from the patient's left side.

The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealing device to remove the gastrosophia fat pad located between the gastric lumen and the left crux of the diaphragm. This action provides exposure of the angle of His, facilitating the dissection of the stomach and preventing a large retained fundus.

The surgeon has completely removed the gastroesophageal fat pad and is now focusing on the lower stomach. The greater curve dissection begins with the identification of the pylorus through palpation and visualization. The surgeon identifies an area on the lesser curve of the stomach, approximately four to six centimeters proximal to the pylorus, to start transecting the greater curved vessels.

The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealing device to seal the greater curve vessels and enters the lesser sac behind the stomach. The device is used to open the space by cutting through connective tissue, allowing access behind the stomach.

The surgeon enters the posterior space and begins transecting the short gastric vessels along the greater curve of the stomach. The spleen is visible, and the short gastric vessels are closely situated between the stomach and spleen. The surgeon proceeds cautiously to avoid tearing these vessels, which could lead to significant bleeding. The goal is to achieve clear mobilization of the greater curve of the stomach without bleeding in the splenic bed, ensuring good visualization and exposure.

The surgeon cauterizes the final attachments on the posterior aspect of the stomach, ensuring clear visualization of the left crus of the diaphragm. This step frees the stomach completely, facilitating the placement of the stapling device for the sleeve gastrectomy.

The surgeon passes a 34 French sizing tube down the esophagus into the antrum of the stomach under direct vision. With the tube in place, the surgeon begins creating the staple line for the sleeve gastrectomy using a robotic stapling device, starting with a green-colored stapler for the thicker portion of the stomach.

The surgeon positions the bougie within the stomach and places the staple line using robotic technology, ensuring adequate tissue compression for proper staple formation. The stapler is fired, and the staple line is inspected, showing no bleeding or serosal tears. The process is repeated vertically along the stomach, transitioning from large to smaller staples as the stomach wall becomes thinner.

The surgeon passes the final staple line across the apex of the stomach, leaving a small dog ear of stomach tissue to ensure the staple line is on the stomach and not the esophagus, which is positioned to the left. The complete staple line of the gastric sleeve is inspected, followed by the use of a robotic needle holder to over-sew the staple line in a running inverting fashion.
The surgeon begins working on the small dog ear of stomach above the staple line, anchoring the Tsuji and inverting the staple line using a running inverting technique.

The surgeon completes the over-sewing of the suture line for the robotic sleeve gastrectomy. They then tack the small dog-ear of the stomach, located to the left of the esophagus, to the left crux of the diaphragm using an interrupted vial suture to prevent herniation into the mediastinum.

The surgeon ties the final suture and removes the stitch from the sleeve gastrectomy. The thirty-four French bougie is extracted, followed by an upper endoscopy to visually inspect the gastric sleeve for any intragastric bleeding or leaks.

The surgeon leaves the robotic console to perform an upper endoscopy. The assistant covers the area with saline to check for air leaks. The endoscope light is visible as it passes through the distended gastric sleeve and antrum, confirming good distention without any air leaks.

The surgeon suctions residual air from the gastric sleeve and removes the endoscope. Fibrin sealant is applied to the suture line to seal sutures and control a small serosal tear on the liver. There is no visible bleeding, and the gastric specimen is then removed.
The surgeon removes the liver retractor, indicating the conclusion of the gastrectomy procedure.
